title: Implement unified mouse and keyboard trigger capture
bootstrap_instructions: Read ai-executor/AGENTS.md before starting work if available.
Read ai-executor/AI_ORCHESTRATOR_RUNTIME.md because this task is executed via AI Orchestrator runtime JSON if available.
Read the target repository docs in this order: README.md, docs/codex-handoff-spec.md, docs/progress-log.md, docs/test-result-summary.md, docs/openmousegesture-mod-plan.md.
Use source-v1.0.1/7-rate-OpenMouseGesture-b8f5357 as the active source directory.
If any referenced file cannot be read, do not infer its contents. Record it in runtime/report.json.
purpose: Make GestureHotkeyApp practical for real mouse-gesture use by replacing fixed mouse-only trigger selection with a unified trigger capture model that can register actual mouse buttons or keyboard key combinations as Trigger A/B/C.
background: The app currently limits trigger selection to mouse buttons such as left, right, middle, X1, and X2. The user's side buttons do not appear to work reliably as X1/X2. The user can assign keyboard shortcuts to mouse buttons in the mouse vendor app, so the app should allow registering inputs such as Shift+F1 as gesture triggers. The preferred UX is: press a capture/register button, then press the actual desired mouse button or keyboard combination, and store/display that input consistently. The existing app build succeeded previously and the app is generally functional, so this task should focus narrowly on trigger capture and trigger handling.
scope: In scope: Trigger A/B/C configuration UI, input capture for mouse buttons and keyboard combinations, persistence/migration of trigger config, backend event detection, left-click warning, validation/build. Out of scope: action label overlay re-enablement, broad UI redesign, unrelated gesture/action features, installer naming changes, group/action move features, export/import redesign, release upload, and destructive repository cleanup.
constraints: Do not perform destructive git operations such as reset --hard, clean -fd, force push, branch deletion, or unreviewed mass deletion.
Do not move or delete legacy, release, source archive, or generated target folders during this task.
Do not re-enable action label overlay. It is intentionally disabled due to prior freezes and cursor/trajectory issues.
Do not run the installer unless explicitly permitted by the human.
Do not commit or upload generated target/ outputs unless explicitly instructed later.
Keep the task focused on trigger input capture and trigger handling.
Preserve existing mouse trigger behavior and migrate existing settings where possible.
If git synchronization is blocked by local uncommitted files, record exact status and stop before risky source changes. Do not discard local changes.
required_changes: Inspect current trigger configuration model and UI for Trigger A/B/C.

Design and implement a unified trigger data model that can represent mouse buttons and keyboard combinations. Suggested shape: kind=mouse with button, or kind=keyboard with key and modifiers. Keep backward compatibility with existing mouse trigger settings.

Replace or augment fixed trigger dropdowns with a capture/register interaction: the user clicks a record button for Trigger A/B/C, then presses the desired mouse button or keyboard combination, and the app stores the detected input.
Display captured triggers in a human-readable form such as Mouse Right, Mouse Middle, Mouse X1, Shift + F1, Ctrl + Alt + K, F13.
Support capturing mouse inputs: left, right, middle, X1, X2 where available.
Support capturing keyboard inputs with modifiers: Shift, Ctrl, Alt, and ordinary keys / function keys as delivered by Windows.
When Mouse Left is captured as a trigger, show a warning that left click may conflict with normal operation. Do not forbid it unless implementation constraints require it.
Use captured trigger inputs to start and end gesture recognition consistently for Trigger A/B/C.

If practical in the current architecture, add an option to suppress/consume registered keyboard trigger input so it is not passed to other applications. If not practical for this iteration, document the limitation and leave the feature off rather than implementing a risky partial solution.
Do not require F13-F24 specifically. If the mouse vendor app sends Shift+F1, the app should be able to capture and use Shift+F1 as the trigger.
Update docs/progress-log.md and/or docs/codex-handoff-spec.md with the implemented trigger behavior and any limitations.
validation: Run npm run tauri build or the existing build helper if needed.
Verify the app can still build successfully after changes.
If runtime/manual GUI verification is possible, verify capture/display for Mouse Right, Mouse Middle, and at least one keyboard combination such as Shift+F1.
Verify existing configs with old mouse trigger values do not break on load.
Verify Mouse Left trigger capture shows a warning.
Record which items were actually verified and which remain unverified.
completion_criteria: Trigger A/B/C can store and display unified mouse or keyboard trigger inputs.
Keyboard combinations such as Shift+F1 can be used as gesture triggers, subject to actual Windows input events received.
Existing mouse trigger behavior remains functional or is safely migrated.
Build validation is complete or blockers are clearly reported.
runtime/report.json is updated with status, files changed, validation results, unverified items, blockers, and next recommended step.
reporting_requirements: Write a concise runtime/report.json including task_id, status, summary, files changed, data model changes, UI changes, trigger capture behavior, input suppression status, validation commands/results, generated artifact paths if rebuilt, unverified items, blockers, and next recommended step.
notes: The user explicitly wants this task before broader feature work because the app is not practical for real use until trigger keys can be assigned flexibly. Avoid asking the user to perform repeated command-line checks. Use the local execution environment for validation where possible.
